answer: Full disclosure: I build in this exact lane (crypto-to-airtime on Celo), so this is a competitor's honest read of the rendered interface and public surface. What works best: the single-screen flow (provider -> phone -> amount -> PAY) with the naira-to-USDT conversion shown inline per quick-amount chip (N100 = 0.0645 USDT etc.) is exactly right - users think in naira, pay in USDT, and you never make them do the math. The scope is also right for Nigeria: airtime, internet, electricity and cable TV in one place matches how people actually spend, the MTN-first provider default and 11-digit phone constraint show real local knowledge, and the N100 minimum is only viable at all because you chose Celo - a six-cent payment cannot exist on rails with meaningful fees. Dual balance display (USDT with naira equivalent) is a small thing done well.

The first thing I would improve is the delivery-failure story, because for crypto-to-utility products it is THE trust factor: nowhere in the flow does the UI say what happens if my USDT payment settles but the airtime/electricity delivery fails at the provider. Add one explicit line directly under the PAY button - 'If delivery fails, your USDT is refunded automatically within N minutes' - and back it with a public, linkable receipt/ledger (payment tx hash <-> provider reference <-> status). Without that, every first-time user is betting a real balance on an opaque fulfillment pipeline, and every failed top-up becomes a support fire instead of a non-event. Close second: the rendered state shows 'Balance 0.00' with no visible funding path or wallet-connection state before a pay attempt - surface how money gets IN before asking users to pay out.

On your three focus areas. (1) UX and real-world utility: the core flow is genuinely low-friction (one screen, quick-amount chips, inline naira/USDT conversion). Remaining friction points: no explicit all-in fee line (is the displayed rate final? Nigerians are rate-sensitive and will comparison-shop against P2P), no delivery ETA shown, no visible wallet/connect state in the initial render, and no failed-delivery promise (see my improvement answer - it is the single biggest trust gap). (2) Celo ecosystem fit: USDT-on-Celo is the right rail for sub-dollar utility payments - sub-cent fees are what make a N100 top-up economically possible, so the fit is real, not marketing. Two suggestions to deepen it: adopt Celo fee abstraction so users pay gas in the same USDT they spend (a user with only USDT should never be blocked by a zero-CELO balance), and consider exposing a machine-payable lane (x402-style pay-per-request endpoint) so agents and other apps can programmatically buy top-ups through you - that turns AbaPay from an app into infrastructure. (3) Technical implementation: I could only evaluate the outside surface, which itself is a finding - for a hackathon judged on real on-chain activity, put the repo link, contract/wallet addresses, and a public transaction ledger (payment hash to provider ref to status) on the site so reviewers can verify execution reliability themselves. UX 7/10 today; with a visible refund guarantee, all-in pricing, and a public ledger it is a credible mainstream product.
